content.text: Why Multimodal RAG?

Real-world documents rarely contain only prose. Technical manuals embed diagrams. Financial reports include charts and tables. Scientific papers present results as figures. Standard RAG discards all non-text content, losing significant information. Multimodal RAG treats images, tables, and diagrams as first-class retrieval targets alongside text: each modality is pre-processed into a retrievable representation (caption, summary, or markdown), embedded into the same vector space, and passed to a multimodal LLM that can reason over both text and images simultaneously.

attrs.alt: Multimodal RAG pipeline: text, images, tables, audio preprocessed into unified vector store, retrieved and passed to multimodal LLM
attrs.title: Multimodal RAG Pipeline

content.text: Extracting and Indexing Images from PDFs

The first challenge in multimodal RAG is extraction: pulling images out of PDFs or Office documents. Unstructured.io's partition_pdf function detects and extracts image elements alongside text elements. Each extracted image is then captioned using a multimodal LLM (GPT-4o or Claude) — the caption becomes the embedding text for retrieval, while the raw image bytes are stored separately for inclusion in the LLM prompt at generation time.

# pip install unstructured[pdf] langchain-openai pillow
from unstructured.partition.pdf import partition_pdf
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
import base64
from pathlib import Path

def extract_and_caption_pdf(pdf_path: str, output_dir: str = "./extracted"):
    """Extract text and images from a PDF; caption each image."""
    Path(output_dir).mkdir(exist_ok=True)

    elements = partition_pdf(
        filename=pdf_path,
        extract_images_in_pdf=True,
        infer_table_structure=True,
        strategy="hi_res",
        image_output_dir_path=output_dir,
    )

    vlm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", max_tokens=256)
    results = []

    for el in elements:
        el_type = type(el).__name__
        if el_type == "Image":
            # Caption the image using VLM
            img_path = el.metadata.image_path
            if img_path and Path(img_path).exists():
                with open(img_path, "rb") as f:
                    img_b64 = base64.b64encode(f.read()).decode()
                caption_response = vlm.invoke([HumanMessage(content=[
                    {"type": "text", "text": "Describe this image in 2-3 sentences for a technical document retrieval system."},
                    {"type": "image_url", "image_url": {"url": f"data:image/png;base64,{img_b64}"}},
                ])])
                results.append({
                    "type": "image",
                    "caption": caption_response.content,
                    "image_path": img_path,
                    "page": el.metadata.page_number,
                })
        elif el_type == "Table":
            results.append({"type": "table", "text": el.metadata.text_as_html, "page": el.metadata.page_number})
        else:
            results.append({"type": "text", "text": str(el), "page": getattr(el.metadata, "page_number", None)})

    return results
Indexing Multimodal Content

All extracted elements are indexed in a unified vector store using text as the embedding input: prose chunks use their text directly, image elements use their VLM-generated captions, and table elements use a markdown or HTML representation. The raw image bytes and full table content are stored separately (in a file store or doc store) keyed by element ID, so they can be retrieved and passed directly to the multimodal LLM at generation time.
Multimodal Generation with GPT-4o

At generation time, retrieved elements are assembled into a multimodal message: text elements are included as plain strings, image elements are included as base64-encoded image_url content blocks, and table elements are included as markdown. GPT-4o and Claude 3+ accept messages with interleaved text and images, allowing the LLM to reason over retrieved visual evidence and provide grounded answers that reference both prose and diagrams.

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
import base64

vlm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0, max_tokens=1024)

def multimodal_rag(query: str, top_k: int = 4) -> str:
    # Retrieve top-k elements (text + images + tables mixed)
    hits = vectorstore.similarity_search(query, k=top_k)

    content_blocks = [{"type": "text", "text": f"Answer using the context below.\n\nQuestion: {query}\n\nContext:"}]

    for hit in hits:
        el_id = hit.metadata["element_id"]
        el_type = hit.metadata["type"]
        raw = raw_store.get(el_id, {})

        if el_type == "text":
            content_blocks.append({"type": "text", "text": raw.get("content", "")})
        elif el_type == "image":
            img_path = raw.get("path", "")
            if img_path:
                with open(img_path, "rb") as f:
                    img_b64 = base64.b64encode(f.read()).decode()
                content_blocks.append({
                    "type": "image_url",
                    "image_url": {"url": f"data:image/png;base64,{img_b64}"},
                })
                content_blocks.append({"type": "text", "text": f"[Image caption: {raw.get('caption', '')}]"})
        elif el_type == "table":
            content_blocks.append({"type": "text", "text": raw.get("content", "")})

    response = vlm.invoke([HumanMessage(content=content_blocks)])
    return response.content

answer = multimodal_rag("What does the architecture diagram show?")
print(answer)
CLIP-Based Image Retrieval

For image-heavy corpora (product catalogues, medical imaging, design libraries), captioning every image is slow and expensive. CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) provides a joint text-image embedding space: you can embed a text query and retrieve the most similar images directly without generating captions first. LangChain's OpenCLIPEmbeddings wraps the CLIP model and can embed both text queries and raw images into the same vector space.

# pip install langchain-experimental open_clip_torch pillow
from langchain_experimental.open_clip import OpenCLIPEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from PIL import Image
import numpy as np

clip_embeddings = OpenCLIPEmbeddings(model_name="ViT-B-32", checkpoint="laion2b_s34b_b79k")

# Index images directly (no caption needed)
image_paths = ["diagram1.png", "chart2.png", "photo3.jpg"]
image_docs = []
for path in image_paths:
    img = Image.open(path).convert("RGB")
    # OpenCLIPEmbeddings accepts URIs; Chroma stores the embedding
    image_docs.append(Document(
        page_content=path,  # URI used as text placeholder
        metadata={"image_path": path},
    ))

image_store = Chroma(embedding_function=clip_embeddings)
image_store.add_documents(image_docs)

# Retrieve images using a text query — CLIP handles cross-modal similarity
results = image_store.similarity_search("flow diagram showing data pipeline", k=3)
for r in results:
    print(r.metadata["image_path"])
Multi-Vector Retrieval for Tables

Tables are a common modality in financial, scientific, and technical documents. The multi-vector strategy indexes table summaries for semantic search while keeping raw HTML or CSV source for context injection. Use an LLM to generate a plain-English summary per table, store the embedding of that summary, and map it back to the original table via a shared document ID.

from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI, OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_chroma import Chroma
from langchain.storage import InMemoryStore
from langchain.retrievers.multi_vector import MultiVectorRetriever
import uuid

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()

# Suppose `tables` is a list of dicts: {"html": ..., "page": ...}
def summarize_table(html: str) -> str:
    prompt = f"Summarize this HTML table for a search index (2-3 sentences):\n{html}"
    return llm.invoke(prompt).content

table_summaries = [(t, summarize_table(t["html"])) for t in tables]

vectorstore = Chroma(collection_name="table_summaries", embedding_function=embeddings)
store = InMemoryStore()
retriever = MultiVectorRetriever(vectorstore=vectorstore, docstore=store, id_key="doc_id")

summary_docs = []
raw_docs = []
for tbl, summary in table_summaries:
    doc_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    summary_docs.append(Document(page_content=summary, metadata={"doc_id": doc_id}))
    raw_docs.append((doc_id, Document(page_content=tbl["html"], metadata={"page": tbl["page"]})))

retriever.vectorstore.add_documents(summary_docs)
retriever.docstore.mset(raw_docs)

# At query time the retriever returns original HTML tables, not the summaries
results = retriever.invoke("quarterly revenue breakdown")
Hybrid Text + Image Retrieval Pipeline

For documents that mix dense text and figures, a dual-retriever architecture works best: one FAISS index for text chunks with OpenAI embeddings, another for image captions with CLIP embeddings, then merge results via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) before passing context to the VLM. This ensures neither modality drowns out the other.

from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.embeddings import HuggingFaceEmbeddings  # CLIP

def rrf_merge(text_results, image_results, k: int = 60):
    """Reciprocal Rank Fusion of two ranked lists."""
    scores: dict[str, float] = {}
    id_to_doc: dict[str, object] = {}
    for rank, doc in enumerate(text_results, 1):
        key = doc.metadata["source"]
        scores[key] = scores.get(key, 0) + 1 / (k + rank)
        id_to_doc[key] = doc
    for rank, doc in enumerate(image_results, 1):
        key = doc.metadata.get("image_path", doc.metadata["source"])
        scores[key] = scores.get(key, 0) + 1 / (k + rank)
        id_to_doc[key] = doc
    return [id_to_doc[k] for k in sorted(scores, key=scores.__getitem__, reverse=True)]

text_store = FAISS.load_local("faiss_text", OpenAIEmbeddings())
image_store = FAISS.load_local("faiss_images", HuggingFaceEmbeddings(model_name="openai/clip-vit-base-patch32"))

query = "show the neural architecture diagram"
text_hits = text_store.similarity_search(query, k=5)
image_hits = image_store.similarity_search(query, k=5)
merged = rrf_merge(text_hits, image_hits)[:6]
Evaluating Multimodal RAG Quality

Evaluation for multimodal RAG extends standard RAG metrics with image-specific signals. Groundedness (does the answer cite a retrieved image/passage?), image relevance (was the image actually needed?), and VLM fidelity (did the model hallucinate details not in the figure?) are the three key dimensions. RAGAS can evaluate the text dimension; image fidelity requires a separate VLM judge.
Operational Considerations
Multimodal RAG pipelines add operational complexity beyond text-only systems. Image storage, captioning cost, CLIP index freshness, and VLM token costs all require explicit attention in production.
Storage:
Store raw images in object storage (S3/GCS) keyed by a content hash; only store embeddings and metadata in the vector DB to avoid bloated indices.
Captioning cost:

GPT-4o captions run ~$0.003/image. For documents with thousands of figures, use a smaller local VLM (LLaVA-7B) for first-pass captioning and only call GPT-4o for images whose caption similarity score is below a threshold.
Index freshness:

When a document is re-uploaded, delete the old image embeddings by doc_id before re-indexing. Use a SQLRecordManager or equivalent to track which image hashes are already indexed and skip re-processing.
VLM context limits:
GPT-4o accepts up to 20 images per request. Cap image context injection at 3–5 images; if more are retrieved, include only the top-scored ones and append their captions as text for the remainder.
Summary
Multimodal RAG indexes text, images (as captions), and tables (as markdown) in a unified vector store.
Use
unstructured
to extract images and tables from PDFs; caption images with a VLM before embedding.
Store raw image bytes separately; include them as base64
image_url
content blocks in the LLM prompt.
For image-heavy corpora, CLIP embeddings enable direct image retrieval without caption generation.
GPT-4o and Claude 3+ accept interleaved text + image messages — pass retrieved images inline for grounded visual reasoning.
